feedback.text: The present act will protect consumers from business overramps in the digital world. It is an area that the right has been tempted to take. This is probably due to the breakthrough of technology over the last three decades. The internet is now a fact and previous legislation has proved unable to honour private individuals. The network is large and anonymous. Then, what is a human being that companies should take her into account? In particular, design that exploits the convenience of individuals (addictive design and disruption when trying to unsubscribe from something) are challenges that this instrument can hopefully overcome. It recalls legislation on cigarettes in the end of the 1900th century. The most effective solution was to ban cigarettes in restaurants and exclusions. The solution seems to be to ban companies’ attempts to manipulate consumers. Although the Act is a good step towards a safer internet, there are further abuses by companies in the digital world. I would like to draw attention to attacks by gambling companies against property rights. The popular movement ‘End dead games’ has more detailed reasoning on the issue, but briefly it can be said that the gambling market (which is not very carefully scrutinised by the media or lawyers) has been transformed over the last 15 years, with more and more games which, in order to function, require a connection to a central server. When companies stop supporting the game, it also ceases to work. Older games have never had this problem, but were manufactured so that the consumer can drive a server himself. Nothing prevents today’s gambling companies from having the same foresight, except that they cost a little more. The games mentioned above are often sold under licences that call them ‘services’. However, it is very unclear when the “service” ends, and the licensing agreement usually claims that the company can terminate the “service” at any time, for any reason (or even none!). Nevertheless, most games are sold as goods: the assumption is that you own it too always! Thus, in the gaming market, the dividing line between goods and services is shaken, with the ultimate aim of ‘owning and being glad’. I would welcome me enormously if this act could be extended to strengthen consumer rights in the gambling market.
